summary:
The primary cytokine implicated in periapical lesion development is interleukin-1 (IL-1), particularly IL-1α and IL-1β, which are produced by activated macrophages and T cells during bacterial infection of root apex tissue. This leads directly to local inflammation and osteoclast-mediated destruction that characterizes chronic apical periodontitis.

The main cytokine responsible for periapical lesion progression is interleukin-1 (IL-1) — specifically IL-1α and IL-1β — secreted by macrophages and T cells upon detection of bacteria at the root tip; this drives sustained inflammation and subsequent alveolar bone loss characteristic of apical periodontitis. 

Final Answer: A

Correct summary adheres exactly to logic presented:

Interleukin-1 (IL-1), especially IL-1α and IL-1β, is central to periapical disease because it is released by activated macrophages and T cells when microbes invade the root apex, triggering localized inflammation and bone resorption via stimulation of osteoclastic activity—a hallmark process in apical periodontitis. Among various pro-inflammatory mediators such as IL-6 and TNF-α, IL-1 emerges most strongly due to consistent associations with initiation and propagation of pathological responses in infected periapical tissues.
